notes: Wife of Cicero, known for her involvement in his public affairs and her strong character.

docs.title: Letter LXI
docs.author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
docs.excerpt: My dear Terentia, most faithful and best of wives, and my darling little daughter, and that last hope of my race, Cicero, good-bye! 29 April, from Brundisium. (1) Cicero says that he has been thirteen days at Brundisium. In the next letter he tells Atticus he arrived on the 17th.

docs.title: Letter LXXVIII
docs.author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
docs.excerpt: … with the greatest courage and most loyal affection, nor am I surprised at it; but I grieve that the position should be such that my miseries are relieved by such heavy ones on your part.

docs.title: Life of Cicero
docs.author: Plutarch
docs.excerpt: … own nature neither tender-hearted nor timorous, but a woman eager for distinction (who, as Cicero himself says, would rather thrust herself into his public affairs, than communicate her domestic matters to him), told him these things, and excited him against the conspirators.
